# User Experience Design — Task Summary

> **This file is generated from W3C WAI ARRM data. Do not edit manually.**
>
> Source: [W3C WAI ARRM](https://github.com/w3c/wai-arrm/blob/draft/_data/arrm/arrm-all-tasks.csv)
> Retrieved: 2025-01-01

## Primary Ownership Tasks (96)

- A mechanism that conveys the way through which the full explanation of complex images is defined. (ID: IMG-011)
- Text content that conveys information is not part of images. (ID: IMG-019)
- iFrames displaying content are provided with a clear, informative title attribute value. (ID: SEM-018)
- iFrames are given a title that describes their content or purpose. (ID: SEM-028)
- Additional content triggered by focus or hover that covers other information can be dismissed by the user. (ID: INP-001)
- Additional content triggered by pointer hover does not disappear when trying to move the pointer over it. (ID: INP-002)
- Additional content triggered by hover or focus stays visible until purposefully un-triggered, dismissed, or is no longer valid. (ID: INP-003)
- Behaviors for hover and focus states are planned and included with the design assets. (ID: INP-007)
- Keyboard focus states are planned for every active element. (ID: INP-008)
- All actionable elements can be reached using only the keyboard without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes. (ID: INP-013)
- Single-key keyboard shortcuts can be disabled or remapped, unless they are only active on keyboard focus. (ID: INP-014)
- Non-essential multipoint, path- or gesture-based functions have single-pointer alternatives. (ID: INP-019)
- Non-essential single-pointer functionality is not triggered on down events, unless the functionality can be canceled, undone, or reversed. (ID: INP-020)
- All non-essential motion-related functionalities have alternative user interface controls that allow for equivalent actions. (ID: INP-022)
- All non-essential motion-related functionality can be turned off to prevent accidental actuation. (ID: INP-023)
- All functionality requiring dragging movements can also be operated using single-pointer alternatives. (ID: INP-024)
- Keyboard focus does not move automatically from one form control to the next. (ID: INP-026)
- Information that is relevant to a form does not appear after the submit button. (ID: FRM-011)
- Data entry fields are designed to autofill previously entered user information when that information is available. (ID: FRM-012)
- The content is designed in a way that does not restrict input modalities available. (ID: FRM-017)
- Changes of context are not initiated automatically as user interface components receive focus. (ID: FRM-018)
- Form interactions are not designed to include automatic changes of context upon input that would otherwise require explicit user action unless previously communicated. (ID: FRM-020)
- Error messages and alerts are visually displayed across the site in a consistent manner. (ID: FRM-021)
- Inline error messages are displayed next to their related form controls. (ID: FRM-023)
- Error messages are grouped as a list at the top of the form. (ID: FRM-024)
- Radio buttons and checkbox labels are positioned to the right of their respective form controls (for left-to-right languages). (ID: FRM-025)
- Related form controls and their respective labels are grouped together visually. (ID: FRM-026)
- Instructions are in close visual proximity to their related controls. (ID: FRM-027)
- Form controls are designed to have persistent visual labels. (ID: FRM-029)
- Placeholder text is not used in lieu of regular text label elements. (ID: FRM-032)
- Text-based instructions are provided to help users correct errors. (ID: FRM-035)
- Users are provided with means to prevent and correct form errors when legal, financial, or data information is involved. (ID: FRM-036)
- When legal, financial, or data information is involved, confirmation screens are provided prior to any final form submission. (ID: FRM-037)
- Context-sensitive help text is available. (ID: FRM-038)
- Users are provided with means to prevent and correct form errors. (ID: FRM-039)
- Except when it is essential for users to re-enter information in the same session, previously entered information is made available through auto-population or user selection. (ID: FRM-040)
- Cognitive function tests that do not rely on object recognition or personal content must provide an alternative non-cognitive method or an assistive mechanism. (ID: FRM-041)
- A cognitive function test during authentication is not required unless an alternative non-cognitive method or an assistive mechanism is provided. (ID: FRM-042)
- Icon fonts used to convey information are provided with a text equivalent. (ID: CSS-001)
- Content is viewable in both portrait and landscape orientation, unless a particular orientation is essential. (ID: CSS-008)
- Elements that receive keyboard focus are at least partially visible in the viewport, without being covered by other content. (ID: CSS-023)
- Elements that receive keyboard focus are fully visible within the viewport, without being covered by other content. (ID: CSS-024)
- Controls with the same functionality or destination are combined within a single target area that is at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels. (ID: CSS-030)
- Additional visual and/or textual cues are provided when color is used to convey information. (ID: NAV-003)
- Users are notified when time limits are about to expire. (ID: NAV-004)
- Options to extend, or even turn off time limits are provided. (ID: NAV-005)
- Users are given means to pause, stop or hide content that automatically updates. (ID: NAV-006)
- Users are provided with means to turn off all updates, except in case of emergencies. (ID: NAV-007)
- Users are provided with means to re-authenticate sessions without loss of data. (ID: NAV-008)
- Users can bypass blocks of content using skip links or similar mechanisms. (ID: NAV-009)
- Skip links (and similar mechanisms) for main content and navigation are provided at the most effective location in the interface (such as the very first tab stop). (ID: NAV-010)
- The functionality and expected destination of skip links and similar mechanisms is clearly defined. (ID: NAV-011)
- A logical and predictable focus order is defined for complex interactions. (ID: NAV-014)
- Multiple mechanisms are provided for wayfinding, such as navigation menus, breadcrumbs, search features, site map, progress bar, steps, etc. (ID: NAV-020)
- Indications are provided to help users identify their current location within the site. (ID: NAV-021)
- Setting the focus to a new element doesn't automatically trigger a context change, such as content updates or the opening of new windows. (ID: NAV-024)
- Interacting with input controls or other equivalent elements doesn't automatically trigger a change of context, unless the user has been notified ahead of time. (ID: NAV-025)
- Navigation mechanisms are repeated consistently throughout the site or application in the same relative order. (ID: NAV-026)
- Links that open new windows visually indicate they will do so. (ID: NAV-030)
- Help mechanisms are repeated consistently throughout the site or application in the same relative order. (ID: NAV-032)
- Table row and/or column headers provide context for data within the table. (ID: TAB-002)
- Data table structure is appropriate for the data being included. (ID: TAB-003)
- Large, complex data tables are broken into smaller, simpler data tables (when possible). (ID: TAB-010)
- Unrelated data is not included in the same data table. (ID: TAB-011)
- All data table header cells are identified. (ID: TAB-017)
- Links to transcript files are provided in close proximity to the audio-only files. (ID: ANM-003)
- The relationship between multimedia files and their associated transcript is clearly communicated through content sequence or focus order. (ID: ANM-004)
- Copy on the page identifies when video content has no sound. (ID: ANM-005)
- When appropriate, further information about the multimedia file is provided in proximity to the multimedia file. (ID: ANM-006)
- Multimedia player controls are provided to turn captions on or off. (ID: ANM-009)
- Controls to toggle audio descriptions features are provided as part of the media player controls. (ID: ANM-015)
- Multimedia player controls are provided to access a version of the video with audio description. (ID: ANM-016)
- Brief descriptions summarizing multimedia content are provided in close proximity to the audio and video files. (ID: ANM-020)
- Multimedia player controls are provided to turn sound on and off. (ID: ANM-022)
- Volume controls for page-level multimedia files are visually located at the top of the page. (ID: ANM-024)
- Prerecorded or live video content is not set to auto-play. (ID: ANM-026)
- Prerecorded audio-only background sounds can be controlled by the user. (ID: ANM-027)
- Multimedia player controls are provided to pause or play the multimedia file. (ID: ANM-030)
- Timing is not an essential part of the event or activity presented by the content. (ID: ANM-031)
- Users are warned that more than 20 hours of inactivity could lead to data loss. (ID: ANM-032)
- Animation features that create the illusion of movement are defined so that users can disable them. (ID: ANM-035)
- Emoticons, emojis, ASCII art, and other non-markup language constructs are supported with equivalent text alternatives and conveyed to assistive technologies. (ID: SCT-001)
- Emoticons, emojis, ASCII art, and other non-markup language constructs are not used as the only way to structure content or convey information. (ID: SCT-002)
- Objects that rely on shape to be properly perceived are supported with additional text information. (ID: SCT-010)
- Objects that rely on size to be properly perceived are supported with additional text information. (ID: SCT-011)
- Objects that rely on visual location to be properly perceived are supported with additional text information. (ID: SCT-012)
- Objects that rely on orientation to be properly perceived are supported with additional text information. (ID: SCT-013)
- Objects that rely on sound to be properly perceived are supported with additional text information. (ID: SCT-014)
- Fully justified text can easily be changed to ragged right text with a simple action. (ID: SCT-017)
- Unusual words, phrases, and abbreviations are organized into a glossary. (ID: SCT-021)
- Unusual words found in the content are linked to their definitions in a glossary. (ID: SCT-022)
- Content is displayed and structured in a way that makes it easier to read. (ID: SCT-028)
- Ambiguous words are supported by a mechanism that helps users identify their specific pronunciation. (ID: SCT-029)
- Fixed-position content does not fully cover focused elements. (ID: DYN-001)
- Fixed-position content does not cover any part of focused elements. (ID: DYN-002)
- Status messages are announced by assistive technologies without affecting the focus. (ID: DYN-003)

## Secondary Ownership Tasks (87)

- Informative alternate text is provided for images (i.e. not "spacer" or image file name). (ID: IMG-001)
- Adjacent linked images and text links pointing the same URL are combined into single links. (ID: IMG-005)
- The purpose or function of complex images is accurately described in text. (ID: IMG-008)
- Images primarily conveying function use alternative text to describe their purpose, rather than what they look like. (ID: IMG-013)
- Text alternatives of dynamically updated images are simultaneously updated as the images change. (ID: IMG-015)
- Alternate means of accessing CAPTCHA information are provided, such as audio CAPTCHA, logical question, or other equivalent means. (ID: IMG-016)
- Charts, graphs, infographics and other visual representations of information don't rely on color alone to convey information. (ID: IMG-018)
- Text that is placed on top of an image is handled semantically through HTML and CSS instead. (ID: IMG-020)
- Unless a particular presentation of text is essential to the information being conveyed, all images that contain text are only used for purely decorative purposes. (ID: IMG-021)
- Informative content is provided through HTML markup. (ID: SEM-001)
- HTML elements are used according to the HTML specification. (ID: SEM-002)
- Navigation groupings are marked up using HTML list or nav elements (or other equivalent means). (ID: SEM-003)
- Header sections are marked up using HTML header elements (or other equivalent means). (ID: SEM-004)
- The main section of a page is marked up using a HTML main element (or other equivalent means). (ID: SEM-005)
- The footer of the page is marked up using a HTML footer element (or other equivalent means). (ID: SEM-006)
- Content that is complementary to the main section is marked up using HTML aside elements (or other equivalent means). (ID: SEM-007)
- HTML elements are used based on the semantics they provide, not based on what they look like. (ID: SEM-008)
- Elements that act as headings are marked up as such. (ID: SEM-011)
- Headings follow a hierarchical sequence without skipping any levels. (ID: SEM-012)
- Headings are marked up using h1 to h6 elements or other equivalent means. (ID: SEM-013)
- Page contains a level 1 heading that describes the page content. (ID: SEM-014)
- Use of native, semantic HTML elements are prioritized over other methods. (ID: SEM-016)
- The source code (or DOM) order matches the suggested visual order of the design. (ID: SEM-017)
- The tab order logically and predictably follows the expected interaction order of the visual design. (ID: SEM-021)
- All actionable elements can be reached, using only the keyboard. (ID: INP-004)
- All active elements can be triggered, using only the keyboard. (ID: INP-005)
- Keyboard focus is not applied to non-active or static elements. (ID: INP-009)
- Custom active elements replicate all inherent keyboard behaviors of native active HTML elements. (ID: INP-010)
- Users can tab through active elements in an order that reflects the intended interaction order of the design. (ID: INP-015)
- Every element that receives keyboard focus is designed to display a visible focus indicator. (ID: INP-017)
- Text or images of text that is usually included in a user interface control is part of its accessible name. (ID: INP-021)
- Text labels and form controls are programmatically associated (using the FOR and ID attributes, or equivalent means). (ID: FRM-002)
- Related form controls are programmatically associated using fieldset and legend elements or other equivalent means. (ID: FRM-004)
- Long option lists in select elements are grouped semantically (using the optgroup element, or other equivalent means). (ID: FRM-005)
- Common group label text is informative, meaningful and provides context for the grouping. (ID: FRM-006)
- Instructions and messages are programmatically conveyed to assistive technologies. (ID: FRM-007)
- Required fields are programmatically conveyed as such to assistive technologies. (ID: FRM-009)
- Instructions on how to use forms are programmatically conveyed to assistive technologies. (ID: FRM-010)
- Data entry fields using the autocomplete attribute for previously entered information are set to the appropriate value. (ID: FRM-013)
- The purpose of user interface components, icons, and regions is implemented using markup languages, in a way that can be programmatically determined. (ID: FRM-014)
- Keyboard focus is dynamically moved to the error message when errors are returned. (ID: FRM-015)
- The purpose of the form control is clearly described in text. (ID: FRM-016)
- Event handlers are not used to automatically trigger a change of context upon input that would otherwise require explicit user action unless previously communicated. (ID: FRM-019)
- Visual indicators are presented to support error messages when errors are returned. (ID: FRM-022)
- Form controls are coded to have persistent visual labels. (ID: FRM-028)
- Clear text-based instructions are provided on how to use the form controls. (ID: FRM-030)
- Instructions provided in forms are displayed in a clear and unambiguous way. (ID: FRM-031)
- Error messages returned provide clear instructions on how to fix them. (ID: FRM-033)
- Instructions intending to prevent errors are provided in text and available. (ID: FRM-034)
- Shape and location are never used as the only way to convey information and relationships between page components. (ID: CSS-006)
- Color is never used as the only way to convey information, context, indicate selection or the presence of errors. (ID: CSS-009)
- The design makes it possible for end users to enlarge the text so that it reflows into a single column without any loss of information or functionality. (ID: CSS-018)
- Instructions are conveyed through more than shape, size, position, or sound alone. (ID: NAV-002)
- Skip links and similar mechanisms point to the expected destination. (ID: NAV-012)
- All active elements receive focus in a logical and predictable order that is prescribed by the visual presentation. (ID: NAV-013)
- Objects that are not actionable are not part of the tabbing order. (ID: NAV-015)
- Focus is sent back to the initiating point when modal dialogs and controls are dismissed. (ID: NAV-016)
- The purpose of each link can be identified in its immediate context, or from the link text alone. (ID: NAV-022)
- Content is logically organized using section headings. (ID: NAV-023)
- Navigational graphics and icons used throughout the site or application are designed to always serve the same function and or meaning. (ID: NAV-027)
- The accessible name of user interface components used across the site or application are defined consistently. (ID: NAV-028)
- Tables are only to be used to lay out tabular information or data. (ID: TAB-001)
- Tabular data and corresponding header cells for that data are part of the same table. (ID: TAB-004)
- The relationship between table header rows and table header columns with data cells in simple data tables is provided through the SCOPE attributes. (ID: TAB-007)
- The relationship between table header rows and table header columns with data cells in complex data tables is provided through the HEADERS and ID attributes. (ID: TAB-008)
- A meaningful description of the structure of data tables is provided. (ID: TAB-015)
- The programmatic order of the table content matches the intended reading order, so not to affect its meaning. (ID: TAB-016)
- Text transcripts are provided for prerecorded audio-only files. (ID: ANM-001)
- Text transcripts are provided for prerecorded video-only files. (ID: ANM-002)
- Synchronized captions are provided for all prerecorded video content. (ID: ANM-007)
- Captions for live audio content are generated using real-time text transcription services. (ID: ANM-013)
- Sign language interpretation is provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media. (ID: ANM-017)
- Extended audio description is provided for all prerecorded synchronized video content when pauses in foreground audio are insufficient to allow audio descriptions to convey the sense of the video. (ID: ANM-018)
- Text alternatives are provided for all prerecorded audio and video files. (ID: ANM-019)
- Live transcripts are provided for all audio content happening in real-time. (ID: ANM-021)
- Volume controls for page-level multimedia files are independent from general computer audio controls. (ID: ANM-023)
- Audio content that automatically starts on page load lasts no longer than 3 seconds. (ID: ANM-025)
- Multimedia player controls can be fully operated using only the keyboard. (ID: ANM-029)
- Animation features that create the illusion of movement support prefers-reduced-motion (or other equivalent means). (ID: ANM-036)
- Information conveyed by multimedia player controls are programmatically announced through assistive technologies. (ID: ANM-037)
- Heading markup is only used for text which acts as a section heading. (ID: SCT-005)
- The headings used in a page provide a logical outline for the document. (ID: SCT-007)
- The intended reading order of the content remains logical when CSS and images are turned off. (ID: SCT-008)
- The source code order reflects the intended reading order of the document. (ID: SCT-009)
- Text content in design assets such as content blocks is left-aligned in left-to-right languages. (ID: SCT-016)
- Ideas conveyed through text are supported with illustrations or other visuals. (ID: SCT-027)
- Status, toast, or similar messages are programmatically determined through WAI-ARIA roles or properties, so they can be presented to assistive technology users without receiving focus. (ID: DYN-004)

## Contributor Tasks (7)

- The purpose or function of complex images is conveyed using a text description, via an alt attribute. (ID: IMG-009)
- Any programmatic grouping of controls has a target area of at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels. (ID: INP-025)
- Users can resize the text on the page up to 200% without any loss of content or functionality. (ID: CSS-013)
- CSS techniques are used to ensure that content doesn't overflow, overlap or get truncated as a result of increasing the text size. (ID: CSS-014)
- Displaying content on narrower screens or magnifying it does not cause multidirectional scrolling. (ID: CSS-019)
- The keyboard focus indicator is clearly visible and has a minimum area equivalent to a 2 CSS pixel thick border around the focused element, except situations where no visible change is required. (ID: CSS-025)
- All active elements receive focus in a logical and predictable order that is prescribed by the visual presentation. (ID: NAV-013)
